trajectory_summary: The call moved in a positive direction — cost-hesitant to cooperative to logistically engaged to booked — but the energy was thin throughout. The rep ran a clean checklist but never built genuine momentum: the bill was captured without being flipped, the core cost anxiety was acknowledged twice and resolved neither time, and the appointment was framed as an in-person visit rather than a phone call. The booking is real but fragile — Robert's biggest concern is still live.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment for the following day at 3:30 PM. However, the appointment carries structural risk: (1) the wife — who is on the title and has the qualifying credit score — was not explicitly confirmed as present for the sit; (2) the rep used 'stop by' language suggesting an in-person visit rather than a phone appointment; (3) Robert's cost anxiety was unresolved at close. The booking is real; its quality is conditional.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Bill anchor + objection inversion — cost anxiety raised twice, flipped zero times
primary_coaching_focus.quote: That's been my biggest concern is I always heard how expensive it was. To for the panels and the batteries and all the things.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Robert named his barrier at the start of the call and again at the end — the same concern bookended the entire conversation without being resolved. The rep had the bill number in hand at 02:35 and a second clean opening at 05:19. Neither became a flip. This is the universal floor gap per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring and coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion, and it's the reason Robert walks into the sit still holding the 'solar is expensive' assumption. The closer will have to do this work. The manager should use the two-touch pattern (00:44 and 05:19) as the coaching anchor — play them back-to-back and build the inversion move from Robert's own words.

key_moments.quote: I've just seen a lot of advertisement about it, and I haven't done it because I figured it'd be expensive to do.
key_moments.stewart_read: Robert hands the rep his barrier in the first substantive line of the call. The correct move per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring is capture + flip: anchor to his current bill pain and invert the cost assumption against it. The rep instead responds with a generic 'no money out of pocket / bill swap' claim — a feature statement, not an emotional anchor. The bill hasn't even been captured yet, so the flip has no number to land on.

key_moments.quote: I think I have, like, a six zero four. My wife has a higher one. She's close to 700, and she's on she's on the mortgage too.
key_moments.stewart_read: Robert confirms his wife is on the title and has the qualifying credit score — she is both the financial qualifier and a co-decision-maker. The rep correctly notes this ('Okay. Awesome. Yeah. So that'll be perfect there.') but does not explicitly confirm the wife will be present for the appointment. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the co-title-holder must be on the appointment. The rep books the sit at 04:38 without revisiting this.

The bill range is captured but the rep's response — 'Dang. Yeah. That's quite the range there. It's crazy.' — is a social acknowledgment, not an anchor. Robert immediately contextualizes the range (new house, gas appliances, uncertainty about summer AC costs), which is actually a forward-looking bill anxiety signal. The rep responds with 'Okay. Sweet. Well, yeah, congrats on the new home.' — rapport, not leverage. The flip opportunity is present and unused.

key_moments.quote: the last thing to do now is to just find a good time for one of our specialists to stop by, just kinda review the information with you
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep uses 'stop by' language, which frames the appointment as an in-person visit. Per button_up.phone_appointment_specified, the appointment is a phone call — this is a hard-required schema element. Robert may now be expecting someone to come to his home. The button-up at 04:48 ('I'll send you a text as well with just the day and time') does not correct this framing.
